title: Charmed Life - Chapter 19 (Fragment 5)
text:
Cat had a very gallant idea. He sent for a blue velvet chair from Gwendolen’s room and put it down on the grass beside Janet. Janet gave a tearful laugh. “That was kind.” She started to sit in it.

“I belong to Chrestomanci Castle,” said the chair. “I belong to Chresto—” Miss Bessemer looked at it sternly and it stopped.

Janet sat in the chair. It was a little wobbly because the grass was uneven. “Where’s Cat?” she said anxiously.

“I’m here,” said Cat. “I got the chair for you.” He thought it was kind of Janet to look so relieved to see him.

“What do you say to a little lunch?” Millie asked Miss Bessemer. “It must be nearly two o’clock.”

“Agreed,” said Miss Bessemer, and made a stately half-turn towards the butler. He nodded. The footman and the gardeners staggered forward with great hampers like laundry baskets which, when the lids were thrown back, proved to be full of chickens, hams, meat pies, ice cream, fruit, and wine.

“Oh, beautiful!” said Roger.

Everyone sat around to eat the lunch. Most of them sat on the grass, and Cat made sure to sit as far away from Will Suggins as he could. Millie sat on the stone slab. Chrestomanci splashed some of the water from the bubbling spring over his face—which seemed to refresh him wonderfully—and sat leaning against the slab. The old lady with mittens produced a tuffet out of nowhere, which she said was more comfortable; and Bernard thoughtfully shook out the remains of the rope that Cat had left by the rock. It became a hammock. Bernard strung it between the pillars of the archway and lay in it, looking defiantly comfortable, even though he had the greatest difficulty keeping his balance and eating as well. Fiddle was given a wing of chicken and took it into the apple tree to eat, out of the way of the dragon. The dragon was jealous of Fiddle. It divided its time between breathing resentful smoke up into the tree and leaning heavily against Cat, begging for chicken and meat pie.

“I warn you,” said Mr. Saunders. “That is the most spoiled dragon in the world.”

“I’m the only dragon in the world,” the dragon said smugly.

Janet was still inclined to be tearful. “My dear, we do understand,” said Millie, “and we’re so very sorry.”

“I can send you back,” said Chrestomanci. “It’s not quite so easy with Gwendolen’s world missing from the series, but don’t think it can’t be done.”

“No, no. That’s all right,” Janet gulped. “At least, it will be all right when I’m used to it. I was hoping to come back here—but it is rather a wrench. You see—” Her eyes filled and her mouth trembled. A handkerchief came out of the air and pushed itself into her hand. Cat did not know who had done it, but he wished he had thought of it. “Thanks,” said Janet. “You see, Mum and Dad haven’t noticed the difference.” She blew her nose furiously. “I got back to my bedroom, and the other girl—she’s called Romillia really—had been writing her diary. She got called away in midsentence and left it lying there, so I read it. And it was all about how scared she had been in case my parents noticed she wasn’t me, and how glad she was when she was clever enough to make sure they didn’t. She was utterly terrified of being sent back. She’d had a dreadful life as an orphan in her own world, and she was miserable
there. She’d written things that made me feel really sorry for her. Mind you,” Janet said severely, “she was just asking for trouble keeping a diary in the same house as my parents. I wrote a note in it telling her so, and I said if she must keep one, she’d better put it in one of my good hiding places. And then—and then I sat there and rather hoped I’d come back.”

“That was kind of you,” said Cat.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows worry or eagerness.
vocabulary.example: Janet said anxiously.

vocabulary.definition: Producing bubbles of gas, especially as a sign of freshness or liveliness.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci splashed some of the water from the bubbling spring over his face.

vocabulary.definition: The chief male servant of a household, in charge of the dining room, wine cellar, and pantry and sometimes also of the entire main floor.
vocabulary.example: She made a stately half-turn towards the butler.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that shows resistance or disobedience.
vocabulary.example: Looking defiantly comfortable, even though he had difficulty keeping balance.

vocabulary.definition: A male servant who attends to the door or carriage of a wealthy household.
vocabulary.example: The footman and the gardeners staggered forward with hampers.

vocabulary.definition: Brave, spirited, noble-minded, or chivalrous.
vocabulary.example: Cat had a very gallant idea.

vocabulary.definition: Swallowed quickly or in large amounts, often indicating nervousness or distress.
vocabulary.example: Janet gulped.

vocabulary.definition: A hanging bed of canvas or netting suspended between two points.
vocabulary.example: Bernard strung it between the pillars of the archway and lay in it, looking defiantly comfortable.

vocabulary.definition: A large basket usually with a lid used for food or laundry.
vocabulary.example: They staggered forward with great hampers like laundry baskets.

vocabulary.definition: A secret or concealed place used for hiding things or oneself.
vocabulary.example: She’d better put it in one of my good hiding places.

vocabulary.definition: Having a tendency or disposition to do something.
vocabulary.example: Janet was still inclined to be tearful.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing envy of someone or their achievements or advantages.
vocabulary.example: The dragon was jealous of Fiddle.

vocabulary.definition: Very unhappy or uncomfortable.
vocabulary.example: She was miserable there.

vocabulary.definition: A child whose parents are dead.
vocabulary.example: She’d had a dreadful life as an orphan in her own world.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or expressing bitterness or indignation at having been treated unfairly.
vocabulary.example: Breathing resentful smoke up into the tree.

vocabulary.definition: Mentally or emotionally affected by painful experiences (implied).
vocabulary.example: She was utterly terrified of being sent back.

vocabulary.definition: In a strict or harsh manner.
vocabulary.example: Janet said severely.

vocabulary.definition: A large, thick, flat piece of stone, concrete, or wood.
vocabulary.example: Millie sat on the stone slab.

vocabulary.definition: In a self-satisfied or complacent manner.
vocabulary.example: The dragon said smugly.

vocabulary.definition: Moved unsteadily as if about to fall.
vocabulary.example: The footman and the gardeners staggered forward with great hampers.

vocabulary.definition: Having a dignified, unhurried, and grand manner; majestic in manner and appearance.
vocabulary.example: Miss Bessemer made a stately half-turn towards the butler.

vocabulary.definition: In a serious, strict, or severe manner.
vocabulary.example: Miss Bessemer looked at it sternly and it stopped.

vocabulary.definition: Full of tears or likely to weep; showing sorrow or emotion.
vocabulary.example: Janet gave a tearful laugh.

vocabulary.definition: Shook involuntarily, typically as a result of anxiety, excitement, or frailty.
vocabulary.example: Her mouth trembled.

vocabulary.definition: A small clump of grass or low seat, such as a small cushioned footstool or low seat.
vocabulary.example: The old lady with mittens produced a tuffet out of nowhere.

vocabulary.definition: Not level or smooth; irregular or patchy in surface or texture.
vocabulary.example: It was a little wobbly because the grass was uneven.

vocabulary.definition: A closely woven fabric with a thick short pile on one side, giving a soft feel.
vocabulary.example: He sent for a blue velvet chair from Gwendolen’s room.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden violent twist or strain; a pang of mental or emotional distress caused by separation.
vocabulary.example: It is rather a wrench.
